title: Binary Trees in Java
summary: Tree anatomy, DFS traversals (in/pre/post/level), height, diameter, BST properties, and Lowest Common Ancestor.

questions.prompt: In-order traversal of a BST produces:
questions.explanation: BST invariant: left < root < right. In-order visits left→root→right, naturally yielding sorted output.

questions.prompt: Pre-order traversal visits nodes in order:
questions.explanation: Root is visited BEFORE its children — useful for copying or serialising the tree structure.

questions.prompt: In-order traversal of this BST: root=4, left=2, right=6:
questions.explanation: In-order: left(2) → root(4) → right(6) → [2, 4, 6].

questions.prompt: Post-order traversal is naturally suited for:
choices: Computing subtree sizes or deleting nodes
questions.explanation: Post-order visits children BEFORE the parent — you have full subtree results before processing the node.

questions.prompt: Height of a single-node tree is:
questions.explanation: Height = number of edges on the longest path to a leaf. A leaf has no edges below it → height 0.

questions.prompt: Height of an empty tree (null) is conventionally:
questions.explanation: Returning -1 for null makes the formula height(node) = 1 + max(h(left), h(right)) work cleanly.

questions.prompt: Height of root=1 → left=2 → left=3 (linear chain)?
questions.explanation: Path: 1→2→3 = 2 edges. height = 2.

questions.prompt: The diameter of a binary tree is:
choices: Longest path between any two nodes (may not pass through root)
questions.explanation: The diameter can arc through any node — its computation tracks the best left-height + right-height + 2 at each node.

questions.prompt: BST validation using only checking node vs its direct parent (e.g. node.val > node.parent.val) is:
choices: Incorrect — must propagate valid min/max bounds down the tree
questions.explanation: A right child's left subtree could violate the ancestor's upper bound. Example: 10→(right)5 is caught by parent but 10→(right)15→(left)6 is valid locally but 6 > 10 is not.

questions.prompt: LCA of nodes 4 and 7 in a BST with root 8, left subtree rooted at 3 is:
questions.explanation: Both 4 and 7 are in 3's subtree (3 < both < 8). Since they split at 6 (left=4, right=7), LCA is 6 — but if tree matches the guide diagram, both are under 6, so LCA = 6.

questions.prompt: In the LCA recursive algorithm, if both left and right recursive calls return non-null, it means:
choices: Current node is below both p and q
p and q are in DIFFERENT subtrees — current node is their LCA
Only one of p/q was found
questions.explanation: When both sides find a target node, the current node is exactly where the paths diverge — it is the LCA.

questions.prompt: Level-order traversal uses a queue rather than recursion because:
choices: Queue gives FIFO order — nodes at depth k are processed before depth k+1
questions.explanation: FIFO order naturally enforces processing one breadth level at a time.

questions.prompt: Snapshotting q.size() before the inner loop in level-order lets you:
choices: Process all nodes of exactly one level per outer iteration
questions.explanation: The snapshot captures how many nodes are on the current level before new children are enqueued.

questions.prompt: A balanced binary tree of n nodes has height approximately:
questions.explanation: Each level doubles the nodes — log₂n levels for n nodes.

questions.prompt: BST search time complexity on a balanced BST:
questions.explanation: Each comparison halves the remaining nodes — identical to binary search.

questions.prompt: A degenerate (skewed) BST has worst-case search complexity:
questions.explanation: Inserting sorted data into a plain BST produces a linked list — all operations become O(n).

questions.prompt: Which traversals can reconstruct a unique binary tree?
questions.explanation: In-order alone or pre/post alone are insufficient — you need in-order paired with another traversal to uniquely reconstruct.

questions.prompt: The path from root to any node in a BST compared to binary search on a sorted array:
choices: Structurally identical — both discard half the search space at each step using the same key comparison
questions.explanation: BST search IS binary search but on a pointer-linked structure instead of an index-addressable array.

questions.prompt: Pre-order of: root=1, left=2, right=3, 2.left=4, 2.right=5:
questions.explanation: Pre-order: root(1) → left subtree pre-order(2→4→5) → right subtree(3) = [1,2,4,5,3].

questions.prompt: Symmetric tree check (mirror of itself) uses:
choices: Recursive check: left.val==right.val AND left.left mirrors right.right AND left.right mirrors right.left
questions.explanation: Mirror symmetry requires comparing outer children (left.left vs right.right) and inner children (left.right vs right.left) simultaneously.

flashcards.back: Left → Root → Right. On a BST this yields sorted ascending output.
Root → Left → Right. Visits root first — use to copy or serialise.
Left → Right → Root. Visits root last — use for subtree-size and deletion.
Best path through node n = leftHeight + rightHeight + 2. Track global max.
ALL left subtree values < node.val < ALL right subtree values.
If both subtrees return non-null → current node is LCA. Else return whichever found something.
Snapshot levelSize = q.size() before inner loop to process exactly one level.

project.brief:
Write TreeLab.java with:
1. inorder / preorder / postorder — return List<Integer>
2. height(TreeNode root) → int
3. diameterOfBinaryTree(TreeNode root) → int
4. isValidBST(TreeNode root) → boolean  (min/max bounds approach)
5. lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode root, TreeNode p, TreeNode q) → TreeNode
6. levelOrder(TreeNode root) → List<List<Integer>>
Include main() tests: a balanced BST, a skewed tree, and a tree where diameter does NOT pass through root.

checklist.text: inorder of a BST returns sorted list
height of null returns -1, single node returns 0
diameterOfBinaryTree uses a single DFS pass with global max (no re-traversal)
isValidBST passes Long.MIN_VALUE / Long.MAX_VALUE as initial bounds
LCA correctly handles p or q being an ancestor of the other
levelOrder returns correct sublists for each depth

hints: Diameter: return height from the recursive helper, update global maxDiameter as a side effect.
isValidBST: use long bounds (not int) to handle Integer.MIN_VALUE and Integer.MAX_VALUE node values.
LCA: the base case 'if (root == p || root == q) return root' handles the ancestor case correctly.
